findings.title: `routerCallNative` can be abused as an arbitrary approved-token spender to drain users with live proxy allowances

best_agent_match.title: Unsafe approve wrappers preserve the classic ERC20 allowance race

rejected_candidates.title: Transfer helper libraries silently accept EOAs or non-contract token addresses as successful transfers
rejected_candidates.reason: The libraries in `interface.sol` are generic vendored helpers, and there is no in-scope callsite showing protocol accounting that trusts these helpers with a user-controlled or misconfigured token address. Without a concrete reachable integration, this is too speculative.

rejected_candidates.title: Unsafe approve wrappers preserve the classic ERC20 allowance race
rejected_candidates.reason: This is a well-known ERC20 caveat rather than a protocol-specific bug here. The only visible approval flow in scope (`FlawVerifier._approveIfNeeded`) resets to zero before setting a new allowance, so the candidate is not supported by an in-scope vulnerable usage.

rejected_candidates.title: ETH transfer helpers forward all gas and can reenter integrating contracts
rejected_candidates.reason: Forwarding gas is not itself a reportable issue; it becomes one only with a concrete caller that updates critical state after the send. No such in-scope usage is present.

rejected_candidates.title: Unsafe fixed-point division helpers return zero on divide-by-zero instead of reverting
rejected_candidates.reason: These helpers are explicitly named `unsafe*` and no sensitive in-scope pricing, accounting, or solvency logic reuses them. Absent a concrete vulnerable callsite, this is only a theoretical misuse warning.

rejected_candidates.title: Minimal proxy library does not verify that the implementation has deployed code
rejected_candidates.reason: `Clones` is a generic library and there is no in-scope factory or deployment path using attacker- or admin-supplied implementations. The candidate lacks a supported exploit path in this codebase.
